coredata.dc:title: Highly efficient Cu-In-Zn-S/ZnS/PVP composites based white light-emitting diodes by surface modulation

Chen, Ting
Xie, Zhixiang
Jiang, Weihui
Wang, Lianjun
Jiang, Wan
Zhang, Xiaojun
coredata.dc:description: Understanding the surface characteristic-optical property relationship of Cu-In-Zn-S (CIZS) quantum dots (QDs) is necessary for tuning the optoelectronic performances, and the photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs) of CIZS QDs prepared in aqueous solution are still relative low compared with that of obtained in organic phase, which seriously restricts their applications. Herein, an efficient method was proposed to prepare CIZS/ZnS/PVP composites and their application in white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) was investigated. CIZS QDs were synthesized through hydrothermal process with the assistance of ionic liquid (IL), which could enhance the PL performance and reduce the reaction time simultaneously. The effects of hydrothermal temperature and time on the phase composition, morphology, and optical properties of CIZS QDs were systematically investigated. The results showed that the BF4 − anion played a crucial role in boosting the emission property of CIZS QDs owing to the effective reduction of dangling bonds on the surface of QDs, which induced by the corrosion of F− deriving from the decomposition of BF4 −. The surface modulation led to the improvement of PLQY from 6.2% to 8.4%. In addition, the PLQY of QDs was further improved to 31.2% by coating with ZnS shell around CIZS cores. Furthermore, highly luminescent CIZS/ZnS/PVP composites were prepared to eliminate the aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching effect, which was beneficial to the application in WLEDs. Consequently, a WLED achieved the color rending index (CRI) of 87.2, correlated color temperature (CCT) of 4977 K, and luminous efficacy (LE) of 90.11 lm/W by using the orange-yellow CIZS/ZnS/PVP composites and green Lu3Al5O12:Ce3+ phosphors as the color conversion layer, suggesting the potential for solid state lighting application.

head.ce:title: Highly efficient Cu-In-Zn-S/ZnS/PVP composites based white light-emitting diodes by surface modulation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Understanding the surface characteristic-optical property relationship of Cu-In-Zn-S (CIZS) quantum dots (QDs) is necessary for tuning the optoelectronic performances, and the photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs) of CIZS QDs prepared in aqueous solution are still relative low compared with that of obtained in organic phase, which seriously restricts their applications. Herein, an efficient method was proposed to prepare CIZS/ZnS/PVP composites and their application in white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) was investigated. CIZS QDs were synthesized through hydrothermal process with the assistance of ionic liquid (IL), which could enhance the PL performance and reduce the reaction time simultaneously. The effects of hydrothermal temperature and time on the phase composition, morphology, and optical properties of CIZS QDs were systematically investigated. The results showed that the BF4 − anion played a crucial role in boosting the emission property of CIZS QDs owing to the effective reduction of dangling bonds on the surface of QDs, which induced by the corrosion of F− deriving from the decomposition of BF4 −. The surface modulation led to the improvement of PLQY from 6.2% to 8.4%. In addition, the PLQY of QDs was further improved to 31.2% by coating with ZnS shell around CIZS cores. Furthermore, highly luminescent CIZS/ZnS/PVP composites were prepared to eliminate the aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching effect, which was beneficial to the application in WLEDs. Consequently, a WLED achieved the color rending index (CRI) of 87.2, correlated color temperature (CCT) of 4977 K, and luminous efficacy (LE) of 90.11 lm/W by using the orange-yellow CIZS/ZnS/PVP composites and green Lu3Al5O12:Ce3+ phosphors as the color conversion layer, suggesting the potential for solid state lighting application.

ce:section.ce:para: The growing concern for environment pollution and fossil energy exhaustion has aroused a soaring demand for more efficient lighting devices. Thanks to the merits of high efficiency, environmental friendliness and durability, WLEDs have been regarded as promising candidates for replacing conventional incandescent bulbs and fluorescent lamps [1,2]. Commercial WLEDs are typically based on the blue LED chips coupling with yellow-emitting Ce3+:YAG phosphors. However, the devices exhibited relative low CRI values of 70 ~ 80 and cold white light with CCT of 5500 ~ 7500 due to the lack of the green and red spectral regions [3–5]. In order to solve the above problems and achieve high-quality lighting, Europium-based red phosphors such as Eu2+:CaAlSiN3 [6], Eu2+:Sr[Li2Al2O2N2] [7], Eu2+:CaS [8] or Eu3+:Y2O2S [9] were proposed to introduce into the WLEDs. Nevertheless, the scattering effect is inevitable in the devices due to the micron size of these phosphors, resulting in the low LE [10]. Moreover, rare-earth phosphors are facing the challenges for their limited spectral tunability and relative low inventory [11,12]. Therefore, developing bright, efficient and inexpensive luminescence materials are necessary to meet the requirement for ideal indoor lighting.

Quantum dots (QDs) have recently drawn widespread attention for the application in WLEDs owing to their high light-conversion efficiency, excellent photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) and tunable emission wavelength [13–15]. Although the well-developed CdSe QDs and CsPbBr3 QDs have been successfully realized the applications in WLEDs [16,17], but the problems of relative high preparation cost and heavy metal pollution limit their large-scale commercial applications. Especially, the Restriction of Hazardous Substances (RoHS) arising from legislation in the European Union directive regulates that the limitation of any Cd- or Pb-containing electrical and electronic device is 100 ppm and 1000 ppm [18]. There is an urgent desire to develop non-toxicity, low cost and highly emission QDs.

The available Cu-In-Zn-S (CIZS) QDs are regarded as potential alternatives for color conversion materials owing to their nontoxicity, large Stokes shift, tunable wavelength and broad emission spectra [19–21]. Recently, considerable efforts have been devoted to the synthesis and theory calculations of CIZS QDs [19–23]. For instance, an efficient heating-up approach was employed to synthesize CIZS QDs at 230 °C under Ar atmosphere, the maximum PLQY of QDs was 78% after coating with ZnS [19]. Later, the PLQY of CIZS/ZnS could be improved to 85% after optimizing the reaction temperature and dopant concentration [20]. It is worth noting that the high synthetic temperature, protective atmosphere and large consumption of organic solvents are inevitable during the organic preparation process, which increases the production cost and hinders their widespread application in WLEDs. Therefore, developing aqueous synthetic techniques have become the research focus in recent years. However, the synthesis of CIZS QDs in aqueous solution is still facing the difficulties of long preparation time and relative low PLQYs [24–27], which seriously restricts their practical applications. Moreover, the fluorescence quenching phenomenon is inevitable for the application of CIZS QDs in WLEDs due to the energy transfer between QDs when the QD solution condenses into solid state [28], which still remains a great challenge to be resolved.

Ionic liquids (ILs) exhibit many unique properties such as less volatile, less flammable, excellent solubility, low toxicity, which has drawn much attention in various field such as batteries [29,30], preparation of inorganic materials [31,32] and catalysis [33,34]. Moreover, ILs have been employed to synthesis of QDs, such as carbon QDs [35,36], InP QDs [37], and CdTe QDs [38]. Recently, we developed an ionic liquid assisted microwave method to prepare water-soluble CIZS/ZnS QDs. The F-containing ionic liquid can be both served as microwave absorbent and fluorine source, which is responsible for the higher PLQY (24.1%) and wider emission tunable range (579 ~ 677 nm) of QDs compared with conventional aqueous preparation [39] . However, the PLQY of QDs is much lower than that of organic phase synthesis due to the low synthesis temperature (95 °C). Therefore, how to further improve the PLQY of CIZS QDs prepared in aqueous solution is still a great challenge. Here, a hydrothermal process was employed to prepare CIZS QDs, which could provide higher reaction temperature for the growth of QDs. Meanwhile, IL 1-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ([Mim]BF4) was introduced into the reaction system to improve the nucleation rate and modulate the surface property of CIZS QDs. The synthesis of CIZS QDs was operated at 100 °C without the requirement of inert gas, high reaction temperature and injection process in contrast to the traditional organic phase synthesis method [19,20]. Due to the lower surface tension of IL compared with water, the instantaneous nucleation rate of QDs was improved, resulting in the decline of reaction time from 5 to 1 h. Meantime, the PL property of CIZS QDs could be improved by introducing [Mim]BF4, indicating its responsibility for both rapid synthesis and bright emission of QDs. The anion BF4 − of [Mim]BF4 played a crucial role on modulating the surface state of QDs, which was testified by comparing with analogous NaBF4. Moreover, the optimal PLQY of QDs increased from 8.4% to 31.2% after coating ZnS shells. Furthermore, the as-prepared CIZS/ZnS QDs were embedded into polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) matrix in order to reduce the aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching effect. Finally, WLEDs were fabricated by combining orange-red emitting CIZS/ZnS/PVP composites and green emitting Lu3Al5O12:Ce3+ phosphors with blue chips. The LE, CRI, and CCT values of WLED were 90.11 lm/W, 87.2, and 4977 K, respectively. This work would provide solid reference for the rapid and efficient synthesis of multiple QDs in aqueous phase and the application in WLEDs.

Copper (II) chloride (CuCl2·2H2O, 99.0%), indium (III) chloride (InCl3·4H2O, 99.0%), zinc (II) acetate (Zn(OAc)2·2H2O, 99.0%), l-glutathione (C10H17N3O6S, 98.0%), sodium citrate (C6H5Na3O7·2H2O, 99.0%), sodium sulfide (Na2S·9H2O, 98.0%), sodium tetrafluoroborate (NaBF4, 99.0%), thiourea (CH4N2S, 99.0%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 99.0%), and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, K30) were purchased from Aladdin. 1-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ([Mim]BF4, 98.0%) was supplied by Linzhou Keneng Material Technology Co., Ltd. China. All reagents were used as received without further purification.

CIZS QDs were prepared by a facile hydrothermal process. In a typical procedure for preparation of CIZS QDs with a Cu:In:Zn = 1:5:1, appropriate amount of CuCl2·2H2O, InCl3·4H2O, Zn(OAc)2·2H2O, SC, and GSH were dissolved in 60 mL deionized water under continuous stirring to obtain homogeneous solution (1.0 mM) , and the molar ratio of n GSH/n Cu+Zn and n SC/n In was 30 and 13, respectively. The pH value of the mixture was adjusted to 9.0 using NaOH solution. Subsequently, a certain amount of [Mim]BF4 was added into the above mixture, while the molar ratio between [Mim]BF4 and cation (n [Mim]/n C) was set as 0 ~ 25. Then, Na2S solution was injected into the solution and stirred for 10 min, followed by transforming into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and maintaining at 100 °C for 1 h. For the samples prepared with the assistance with NaBF4, the [Mim]BF4 was replaced with NaBF4, while other experimental conditions remained the same.

After the above CIZS core QDs cooled down the room temperature, the appropriate amount of Zn(OAc)2·2H2O, thiourea, and GSH (n S/n Zn = 1, and n GSH/n Zn = 1.5) dissolved in 2 mL deionized water were added into the crude solution, and the pH value of the solution was adjusted to 9.0 by adding NaOH solution. Afterwards, the mixture solution was transformed into Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and heated for 2 h at 100 °C to deposite ZnS shell on the surface of CIS cores. Finally, the CIZS/ZnS QDs solution was cooled naturally.

For the preparation of CIZS/ZnS/PVP composites with a range of concentrations (mass ratio of PVP:CIZS/ZnS QDs solution from 1:100 to 5:100), a certain amount of PVP was added into 20 mL aqueous solution of CIZS/ZnS QDs and treated by ultrasonic for 20 min. Afterwards, the mixture was dried in an oven at 60 °C for 4 h. The dried blocks were grinded for 15 min in agate mortar to obtain well dispersed CIZS/ZnS/PVP composites.

The unpackaged blue-emitting LED chips (λ = 460 nm) were used to fabricate the WLED devices. In order to obtain practical devices, the as-prepared orange-red emitting CIZS/ZnS/PVP composites and commercial green emitting Lu3Al5O12:Ce3+ phosphors were mixed in a certain amount of heated curable silicone, and the mass ratios of orange-yellow/green phosphors were 1/1, 2/1 and 3/1, respectively. The mixture was placed into a vacuum oven to remove the bubbles. Subsequently, the mixture was deposited on the blue chips and cured at 120 °C for 30 min.

Photoluminescence (PL) and ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded using Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer and PerkinElmer Lambda850 spectrometer, respectively. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured by D8 Advance (Bruker) diffractometer using Cu Kα as radiation source (λ = 1.54056 Å) with 2θ range of 10 ~ 70°. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were performed using JEOL JEM-2010 transmission electron microscope with an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. Energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) was employed to study the elemental composition of QDs using a scanning electron microscope (Hitachi SU8010) equipped with IXRF systems. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Nicolet 5700) was employed to identify the surface character of QDs using KBr pellets. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out on a PHI Quantera SXM spectrometer using monochromatised Al Kα radiation. The PL kinetics was recorded on the Fluorolog-3 spectrofluorometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon) with excitation at 374 nm (the pulse duration is below 1.0 ns). The PLQY was calculated using Rhodamine 6G in ethanol as reference (QY = 0.95 at 488 nm), and the absorbance of all samples was diluted to below 0.1 in order to eliminate the reabsorption effects. Temperature-dependent PL spectra ranging from 25 to 200 °C were recorded using Edinburgh FLS1000 spectrometer equipped with a temperature controller. The photoelectric properties of the fabricated WLED devices were measured by an integrating sphere spectroradiometer system (HP8000, HongPu Optoelectronics Technology Co., Ltd.).

Hydrothermal temperature plays an important role on the phase and optical properties of QDs through changing the nucleation and growth processes. Fig. 1 shows the XRD patterns of CIZS QDs prepared at different reaction temperature via IL assisted hydrothermal method. All patterns present broad diffraction peaks, indicating the ultrasmall size of QDs [28]. There are three obvious peaks at 2θ = 28.0°, 46.5°, and 55.3°, which show a slight shift toward higher angles compared with the (112), (204), and (312) planes of chalcopyrite CuInS2 (JCPDS 27-0159) due to the decrease of the lattice constant, related to the incorporation of the smaller radius Zn2+ into the crystal lattice [40]. Moreover, the diffraction intensity gradually increases with increasing the reaction temperature owing to the improvement of crystallinity. Unfortunately, the samples prepared at 120 ~ 130 °C display additional diffraction peaks at 2θ = 20.0°, which can be assigned to the impurity In2S3 (JCPDS 21-0413) deriving from the decomposition of ligand and the relative high reactivity of In3+ at high temperature [41].

Fig. 2 shows the TEM images of CIZS QDs prepared under 90 ~ 110 °C. It is obvious that all the samples are near-spherical shape and well-dispersed. The average diameter of CIZS QDs prepared at 90, 100, and 110 °C were 2.33, 2.62, and 2.95 nm, respectively, demonstrating an increase in size with the evaluated reaction temperature. The HRTEM images reveal a relative high crystalline of the prepared QDs with continuous lattice fringes throughout the whole particle, indicating that the crystalline of CIZS QDs can be improved after adding IL during hydrothermal process compared with conventional aqueous preparation method [26]. The fringe space was about 0.32 nm, which was corresponded to the (112) plane of CuInS2 [40].

Fig. 3 shows the PL performance of CIZS QDs prepared at different temperature. The PL intensity of QDs was significantly enhanced with increasing the reaction temperature from 90 to 100 °C. It could be attributed to the fact that higher reaction temperature promoted the rearrangement of surface atoms, resulting in the decrease of surface defects [15]. However, the PL intensity gradually declined with further increasing the reaction temperature, because the fast reaction growth rate caused the formation of numerous non-radiative defects [42]. Moreover, the emission peak of CIZS QDs gradually shifted from 639.4 to 656.4 nm owing to the increase of the particle size, which weakened the quantum confinement effect [43]. However, it could be found that a unique emission peak centered at 430 nm caused by the phase separation of In2S3 when the reaction temperature was beyond 120 °C [44], which is in good agreement with the XRD analysis in Fig. 1.

The effect of reaction time on the formation of CIZS QDs was further investigated. Crystal structures of CIZS QDs prepared at 100 °C under different reaction time were determined by XRD, as shown in Fig. 4 . The diffraction patterns of the samples prepared within 5 h showed a similar zinc blend structure, and their characteristic peaks were located between the distinct diffraction peaks of CuInS2 (JCPDS 27-0159) and ZnS (JCPDS 05-0566), which indicated the formation of quaternary CIZS QDs [45]. Furthermore, the diffraction intensity of samples gradually intensified with prolonging the reaction time, demonstrating the improvement of crystallinity of CIZS QDs. Nevertheless, the impurity diffraction peaks of In2S3 phase were observed with further prolonging the reaction time to 7 and 9 h, which were also owing to the decomposition of ligands under long holding time [41].

Fig. 5 shows the TEM images of CIZS QDs prepared at 100 °C under different reaction time, and the particle size distributions of QDs could be calculated from TEM images. All the samples exhibited quasi-spherical shape and a significant increase in particle size with prolonging the reaction time. The statistical results indicated that the average size of CIZS QDs increased from 2.68 to 3.35 nm with prolonging the reaction time from 1 to 5 h.

Fig. 6 shows PL spectra and evolution of PL intensity and peak position of CIZS QDs prepared at 100 °C under the reaction time of 0.5 ~ 9 h. The emission peak of QDs exhibited a red-shift tendency from 622.0 to 677.4 nm with increasing the reaction time due to the increase of particle size, which could be confirmed by TEM results (Fig. 5). Moreover, the PL intensity exhibited a remarkable enhancement with prolonging the reaction time and reached the maximum value at 1 h, which was attributed to the completion of CIZS crystal structure [41]. It was worth noting that the reaction time in this approach was much shorter than other hydrothermal process [24,46], because the low surface tension of IL effective enhanced the instantaneous nucleation rate of QDs [47] . However, the emission intensity gradually declined with further increasing the reaction time, which could be attributed to the fact that the oxidized ligands loose contacted with QD surface [48]. Meanwhile, the additional emission peak at 430 nm deriving from In2S3 could be observed under excessive reaction time (7 h and 9 h), which was well in accordance with the XRD results in Fig. 4.

In order to further study the effect of IL on the growth of CIZS QDs, Fig. S1 shows the XRD patterns of CIZS QDs prepared at 100 °C under different reaction time in the absence of IL. The pure CIZS phase could be obtained in the reaction time range from 1 to 7 h, while In2S3 phase was formed at 9 h. The PL spectra of CIZS QDs prepared under different reaction time are shown in Fig. S2. Similarly, the emission intensity of CIZS QDs progressively enhanced along with the increase of reaction time, and the highest PL intensity was acquired for the sample with the reaction time of 5 h. It is worth noting that the optimal reaction time decreased sharply from 5 h to 1 h after adding IL. This might be attributed to the lower surface tension of IL [Mim]BF4 compared with water, which could effective enhance the instantaneous nucleation rate of QDs [47]. Furthermore, the emission intensity evolution of CIZS QDs prepared with and without the assistance of IL is shown in Fig. S3. The maximum emission intensity of QDs prepared with the assistance of IL was much stronger than that of the IL-free control sample, demonstrating that the introduction of IL into the hydrothermal reaction provides a feasible approach for the efficient synthesis of high-performance water-soluble CIZS QDs.

The effect of IL addition amount on the phase and optical property of CIZS QDs was further explored. The crystallographic characteristics of CIZS QDs with different n [Mim]/n C ratios were determined by XRD, as shown in Fig. 7 . According to the XRD patterns, the diffraction peaks of samples were both located between the bulk CuInS2 and ZnS, which demonstrated the fact that the prepared QDs were not consist of a mixture of CuInS2 and ZnS, suggesting the formation of alloy CIZS QDs [49]. Moreover, it was clearly shown that the diffraction peaks of CIZS QDs gradually became wider with the increase of n [Mim]/n C ratios, which indicated that the particle size of sample was reduced gradually.

TEM was used to track the morphological changes of QDs with different n [Mim]/n C ratios. As shown in Fig. 8 , the QDs are near-spherical shape and monodisperse. The average size of QDs with n [Mim]/n C = 0, 10, 20 was calculated as 4.27 nm, 2.65 nm and 2.35 nm, respectively. This might be attributed to the fact that the low surface tension of [Mim]BF4 facilitated the nucleation of QDs [50,51]. Moreover, the size distribution of QDs was narrowed with the increase of n [Mim]/n C ratio, indicating that the addition of IL could effective modulate the morphology of CIZS QDs.

Fig. 9 presents the emission property of CIZS QDs with different n [Mim]/n C ratios. The emission intensity of CIZS QDs was gradually enhanced with the increase of n [Mim]/n C ratios from 0 to 10, while the corresponding PLQY of QDs increased from 6.2% to 8.4% (Fig. S4 and Table S1), indicating that the emission performance could be effective improved after adding a certain amount of [Mim]BF4. Moreover, the peak position of QDs exhibited blue-shift from 633.4 to 621.8 nm. This could be attributed to the fact that the surface tension of the [Mim]BF4 was much lower than that of water [50]. Therefore, the surface tension of the mixture solution was lowered after adding IL, which facilitated the nucleation of QDs, resulting in the decreased particle size [51].

To elucidate the bonding mechanism between [Mim]BF4 and QDs, FT-IR was employed to examine the presence of groups in samples prepared with and without adding IL. As shown in Fig. 10 , the FI-IR spectrum of [Mim]BF4 presents a series of characteristic peaks, the peaks at 3150 cm−1, 2850 cm−1, and 1300 cm−1 are assigned to the C–H asymmetric stretching vibration of imidazole ring, and the peak at 2960 cm−1 corresponds to the symmetrical stretching vibration of C–H [52]. The peaks at 1580 cm−1 and 1150 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching modes of C–N+ of imidazole ring, and the peak at 1060 cm−1 is related to the B–F asymmetric stretching vibration of BF4 − [53]. The FI-IR spectrum of the QDs prepared with the assistance of [Mim]BF4 shows the C–N characteristic mode at 1580 cm−1 and 1150 cm−1, indicating the presence of [Mim]+ on the surface of QDs. However, the characteristic peaks deriving from C–N bond are absence in the sample without adding the IL, which further verifies that the [Mim]+ cation of IL bonds with CIZS QDs. Moreover, the B–F asymmetric stretching vibration at 1060 cm−1 disappears in the sample with the assistance of [Mim]BF4, indicating that the B–F bond is broken during the hydrothermal process [54].

Furthermore, XPS was used to probe the chemical composition of CIZS QDs. As shown in Fig. 11 , the electronic signals of Cu, In, Zn and S can be observed, indicating the presence of these elements. The values of binding energy suggest that the cations of Cu, In as well as Zn are in the valence state of +1, +3 and +2, respectively [24]. As can be seen in Fig. 11 (e), the S 2p peak can be fitted into two peaks with binding energies of 162.5 eV (S 2p1/2) and 161.3 eV (S 2p3/2), which shows the energy separation of 1.2 eV, demonstrating that the valence state of S is −2 [24]. Moreover, it is worth noting that the F 1s peak at 685.1 eV can be observed in the CIZS QDs prepared with the assistance of [Mim]BF4 [55], indicating the presence of F− on the surface of the QDs. In contrast, the IL-free control sample does not show the electronic signal of F−. These results presented above clearly showed that the B–F was broken during the hydrothermal process and then released F−. Furthermore, the compositions of samples were determined by EDS, as shown in Table S2. The atomic ratios of Zn:Cu for the samples prepared with different n [Mim]/n C ratios were close to 1, which was consistent with the molar ratios in the precursor. However, the In:Cu ratios were in the range of 3.80 ~ 4.01, which was smaller than the feed ratio (In:Cu = 5) due to the lower chemical reactivity of In3+ at low temperature [56]. It was worth noting that the content of F gradually enhanced along with the increase of the n [Mim]/n C ratios, testifying the formation of F− during the reaction.

To further demonstrate the optical properties of the CIZS QDs prepared with different addition amount of [Mim]BF4, their fluorescence dynamics were investigated and presented in Fig. 12 . The PL decays of the CIZS QDs with different n [Mim]/n C ratios can be well fitted by bi-exponential function I(t) = A 1 exp(−t/τ1) + A 2 exp(−t/τ2), where A represents the relative fractional contribution of PL decay lifetime (τ). The corresponding fitting parameters are summarized in Table 1 , and the average lifetime (τave) can be calculated by the function τave = (A 1τ1 2 + A 2τ2 2)/(A 1τ1 + A 2τ2). It is reported that the faster lifetime can be assigned to surface trap states, whereas the slower lifetime is ascribed to the donor-acceptor related transition [57]. The fast decay component (A 1) of samples was decreased from 29.71% to 22.19% with the increase of n [Mim]/n C ratios from 0 to 10, indicating that the surface defects of QDs were effective passivated after adding [Mim]BF4, which could be attributed to the removal of surface dangling bonds by F− releasing from BF4 − [47]. Meanwhile, the average lifetime increased from 280.17 to 304.14 ns, further demonstrating the well passivation of trap states. However, the value of A 1 was increased to 27.25% with further increasing the n [Mim]/n C ratios due to the surface distortion of QDs originating from the corrosion by excess F− [47]. These results illuminated that the introduction of [Mim]BF4 was beneficial to improve the emission performance of CIZS QDs.

While the [Mim]BF4 assisted hydrothermal method is very effective in boosting the luminescence performance of CIZS QDs, it is of great interest to examine whether [Mim]BF4 is unique or just a series of a class of performance-enhancing additives. Therefore, an analogous inorganic salt NaBF4 was selected as F− containing additive to prepare CIZS QDs, while the other experimental conditions maintained invariably. As shown in Fig. S5, all the samples prepared with different molar ratio of n NaBF4/n C are pure CIZS, which indicates that the addition of NaBF4 does not affect the formation of CIZS QDs. The emission intensity (Fig. S6) of QDs was gradually increased with the increase of n NaBF4/n C ratios and reached the maximum value at n NaBF4/n C = 10, demonstrating that a proper amount of NaBF4 was beneficial to improve the emission performance of CIZS QDs. Fig. S7 shows the FT-IR spectra of CIZS QDs and NaBF4, the disappearance of the B–F asymmetric stretching vibration at 1060 cm−1 for CIZS QDs suggests the decomposition of BF4 − [55]. Meanwhile, the appearance of F 1s signal in XPS spectra (Fig. S8(f)) for the sample with the assistance of NaBF4 further confirms that the B–F bond was broken during the hydrothermal process. Moreover, the decay curves of CIZS QDs with different n NaBF4/n C ratios are shown in Fig. S9, and the fitting parameters by biexponential function are shown in Table S3. It can be seen that the fast decay component decreases from 29.71% to 24.65% with the increase of n NaBF4/n C ratios from 0 to 10, demonstrating the passivation of surface defects. On the basis of the above results, it can be concluded that the F− is released from the decomposition of BF4 − during the hydrothermal process, which can etch the surface dangling bonds of QDs effectively, resulting in the significant enhancement of PL intensity. However, the PLQY of CIZS QDs prepared with the assistance of [Mim]BF4 (8.4%) was higher than that of the sample with adding NaBF4 (6.5%). This could be attributed to the fact that the organic cation of [Mim]BF4 was bonded with QD surface, which could also passivate the surface defects of QDs.

In order to further improve the PLQY of QDs, a wide bandgap material ZnS was employed to coat on the surface of CIZS QDs. Fig. S10 shows the XRD patterns of CIZS/ZnS QDs with different n Zn/n CuInZn ratios. The diffraction peaks continuously shifted toward larger angles with increasing the n Zn/n CuInZn ratios, indicating the formation of CIZS/ZnS core/shell structure [58]. Furthermore, the PL intensity of QDs could be significant improved by coating ZnS shell (Fig. S11), and the maximum PLQY of CIZS/ZnS QDs could reach to 31.2% at n Zn/n CuInZn = 3.0 (Table S1).

The superior emission property of CIZS/ZnS QDs motivates us to investigate their application prospects in lighting field. Unfortunately, it is well known that the aggregation-induced emission quenching phenomenon is usually inevitable when the QD solution condenses into solid state due to the energy transfer between QDs [59], which limits their application in WLED. To overcome this problem, we prepare CIZS/ZnS-based phosphors through embedding CIZS/ZnS QDs into PVP matrix. As shown in Fig. 13 (a), the PL intensity of CIZS/ZnS/PVP composites gradually enhanced with the increase of PVP/QDs mass ratios due to the elimination of quenching effect [28]. It is noted that the PL intensity of CIZS/ZnS/PVP composites are significant increased compared with that of CIZS/ZnS powders. Moreover, more than 1 g of neat powders can be obtained in a batch (Fig. 13(b)), indicating the mass production of composites. The CIZS/ZnS/PVP composites emitted bright orange-yellow light under a UV lamp (Fig. 13(c)), demonstrating the excellent emission performance of the composites. In order to further evaluate the emission performance of CIZS/ZnS/PVP composites, the thermal stability of the composites in temperature range from 25 to 200 °C was investigated. Fig. S12(a) and (b) present the temperature-dependent emission spectra and emission intensity map of the sample prepared with the PVP:QD solution mass ratio of 1:50. It can be seen that all emission peaks located at the same positions with the maximum peak at 613 nm. Moreover, the emission intensity was gradually declined with the increase of operating temperature and maintained around 52.5% of the initial value at 125 °C, demonstrating its application prospect in WLEDs.

To investigate the device performance of the prepared composite phosphors, WLEDs were fabricated by combining blue chips with orange-yellow CIZS/ZnS/PVP composites and green Lu3Al5O12:Ce3+ phosphors. The CIE coordinates of three WLEDs with the CIZS/ZnS/PVP loading amount of 0.1 g (Sample A), 0.2 g (Sample B), and 0.3 g (Sample C) were marked in Fig. 14 (a), and the detail parameters of WLEDs were listed in Table S4. It was worth noting that the CCT of devices decreased from 6139 to 4461 K with the increase of CIZS/ZnS/PVP loading amount, corresponding to the increase of CRI from 80.5 to 88.3, which could be attributed to the effective compensation of red light region in the spectrum. Moreover, the photographs of WLEDs driven at 20 mA were shown in the inset of Fig. 14(a). The light emitted by the devices changes from cold to warm, which is well in accordance with the above results. Fig. 14(b) presents the EL spectra of Sample B, and the emission range of WLED covers most of the visible region. The LE of Sample B was 90.11 lm/W, which was comparable to some state of the art QD-based WLEDs (Table 3) [58–67]. Furthermore, the evolution of EL spectra under an increasing bias current was measured. The emission intensity of Sample B gradually enhanced with increasing the bias current from 20 to 100 mA (Fig. 14(c)). The WLED exhibited excellent performance as shown in Fig. 14(d)–(f) and Table 2
. The CRI value of the device decreased from 87.2 to 84.2 with increasing the drive current, while the CCT ranged from 4977 to 5222 K. And the LE of the device showed a decrease trend from 90.11 to 81.11 lm/W. The CIE coordinates at 20 mA (0.349, 0.356) were not very different from those at 100 mA (0.342, 0.350). The above analysis demonstrates that the prepared CIZS/ZnS/PVP composites are good candidates for WLEDs.

In summary, we developed a novel IL assisted hydrothermal approach to prepare water-soluble CIZS QDs. It was found that the reaction time was significant reduced with the assistance of [Mim]BF4. The emission intensity of CIZS QDs gradually enhanced with increasing the n [Mim]/n C ratios, and reached the maximum value at n [Mim]/n C = 10. Moreover, the surface property studies revealed that the dangling bonds on the surface of QDs could be passivated by the etching of F− released from the decomposition of BF4 − during the hydrothermal process, resulting in the decrease of fast decay component and increase of PLQY from 6.2% to 8.4%. Furthermore, the PLQY of CIZS QDs prepared with the assistance of [Mim]BF4 was higher than that of the sample with adding NaBF4 owing to the passivation of surface defects by organic cation of [Mim]BF4. Coating with the ZnS shell on the surface of CIZS QDs was employed to further passivate the surface defects, leading to a significant enhancement of PLQY to 31.2%. The problem of aggregation-induced solid state emission quenching can be effective resolved by preparing CIZS/ZnS/PVP composites. WLEDs were fabricated by combining orange-yellow CIZS/ZnS/PVP composites and green Lu3Al5O12:Ce3+ phosphors with blue chips. The optimal device exhibited a high LE of 90.11 lm/W and a CRI of 87.2. This work will open up new avenues for the efficient preparation of high performance water-soluble CIZS QD based warm WLEDs. We anticipate that further enhancing the PLQY of CIZS QDs and regulating their emission toward red light direction will make greater progress in WLEDs, and the corresponding researches are underway in our group and the results will be reported in due course.
